FilesAnalysis_38393166.description:
Determines the fields which should be analyzed and how the fields should be analyzed.

Let's go through the keyword option from the example: At first the filter is applied to the files (if a filter is specified in the request). The filtered files are then analyzed. For the keyword option, the analyzer looks for the keywords, which occur the most in the resulting files. The keywords are ordered by number of occurrences in the response.

A result for the keyword option in the example could be:

test 593 tester 255 testcase 188

The settings are interpreted as follows in the example: - limit: return 3 keywords maximally - mincount: the returned keywords must occur in at least 5 files - prefix: the returned keywords must have the prefix "test"

Only if showAnalysis: true


required if: showAnalysis == true
FilesAnalysis_38393166.title: FilesAnalysis

info.description:
Data privacy statement · Legal notice   **Welcome to the Documentation of the pixx.io REST API!** The API provides a collection of HTTP endpoints, that can be used for creating, updating, and retrieving data within your pixx.io workspace. The API expects PUT and POST requests to be of the content-type application/json or application/x-www-form-urlencoded unless you send a file with the requests, in which case it has to be a multipart/form-data request. All API responses are in JSON. This documentation serves as a comprehensive guide to help you integrate with the API and leverage its functionalities to enhance your applications and workflows. The following sections explain some basic concepts about the API, further down you find all available endpoints and their documentation. # General Information
Authentication

Authentication

pixx.io relies on HTTP Bearer Authentication for all API requests. The Token comes in the form of an API key, which can be obtained from within your Mediaspace (Settings → API → API-Key → Generate). In order to authenticate, the API Key needs to be sent with every API request in the Authorization Header like this: Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}.
The API Key is bound to the user that generated it, meaning that all requests (e.g. File Uploads or Downloads) are treated like they come from the user that generated the API Key.

Note: Currently only pixx.io users with admin rights have the ability to create an API Key.

Example

curl --location \
'{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/collections?page=1&pageSize=20' \
--header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

Transition from Old Authentication

Note: During the API Beta Phase, we were using a more complicated approach that involved a (Beta-)API-Key, Refresh- & AccessTokens.

This approach has been deprecated in the meantime, please consider switching to Bearer Authentication.

To switch from the deprecated Refresh- & AccessToken authentication method to Bearer authentication, follow these steps :

  1. Create a Bearer Token using the API-Key Section in your pixx.io mediaspace.
  2. Remove any parts of your script that deal with RefreshToken and AccessToken.
  3. Instead of using AccessToken, use the new Bearer Token for your requests in the Authorization Header.

Remember, the format of your request "Authorization"-header changes from Key {your_access_token} to Bearer {your_api_key} during this transition.

API Keys: Best Practices

API keys are an integral part of secure application development, serving as a means to authenticate and authorize requests to an API. However, if not managed properly, they can pose serious security risks. Here are some best practices for handling API keys:

  • Restrict IP Addresses: Make use of the ability to restrict the IP addresses from which the API key can be used. This adds an extra layer of security by ensuring that even if an API key is compromised, it can't be used from an unauthorized location.
  • Rotate API Keys Periodically: Regularly changing your API keys can help prevent unauthorized access if a key is compromised. It is recommended to rotate API keys at least every six months, or whenever significant personnel/system changes occur, or after any known or potential compromises.
  • Do Not Store API Keys in Code Repositories: API keys should not be stored in files that get checked into your application code repository. Instead, store keys in environmental variables or use secure storage solutions.
  • Limit the Scope of an API Key: Use different API keys for different applications to limit the scope of each key. This practice not only enhances security but also makes it easier to monitor and manage API usage. For example, you can create up to 50 API keys for your system.
  • Share API Keys Securely: API keys can be viewed and created by users with admin rights in pixx.io. When sharing keys with non-admins, do so using a medium that is secure, has appropriate access restrictions, and is destroyed after key sharing. This ensures that the keys are only accessible to authorized individuals and are not left exposed where they could potentially be accessed by unauthorized users.

By following these best practices, you can significantly enhance the security of your API keys and protect your system from unauthorized access or data breaches.

Error Handling

Error Handling

When something goes wrong, our API responds with an error message structured like this:

{
  "success": false,
  "errorGroup": "unknown",
  "errorCode": 2354,
  "errorMessage": "The required parameter accessToken is missing."
}

This error message is made up of:

  • success: Tells you whether your request was successful (in this case, it's not).
  • errorGroup: Categorizes the error, helping us understand what went wrong.
  • errorCode: A unique code assigned to the specific error (primarily for internal use).
  • errorMessage: A description of the error in a language you can understand.
⚠️ Note: While errorCode is provided, it is primarily intended for internal purposes. You should focus on the returned HTTP Status Code and the errorMessage to understand and address the error.

Possible HTTP Status Codes:

Situation HTTP Status Code What It Means
Successful Request200Success: Your request worked perfectly.
Created201Success: Your request has been successfully processed and a new resource has been created.
No Content204Success: Your request was successful, but there is no content to return in the response body.
Malformed Request400Error: Your request is a bit off or contains wrong information.
Authentication Issue401Error: Your request needs proper authentication or the given credentials aren't right.
Access Denied403Error: The request is valid, but you're not allowed to do that.
Resource Not Found404Error: We couldn't find what you're looking for.
Unsupported Action405Error: Your request uses a method that's not supported for this particular resource.
Data Conflict409Error: There's a clash, usually because the data already exists.
Too Many Requests (Rate Limit)429Error: Your request is a bit too frequent. Check the Rate Limit guide in this documentation.
Internal Server Issue500Error: Something's not right on our end. If you keep running into this error, contact support.
Functionality Not Ready501Error: We haven't gotten around to implementing that just yet.
Temporary Unavailability503Error: We're taking a short break for maintenance or because things got busy.
Rate Limits

Rate Limits

We've put two different types of rate limits in place to keep things fair and running smoothly.
Rate Limits are shared between all API Keys of your system.

  1. 10 Seconds Rate Limit: Up to 167 requests per system within a 10-second window. This window starts when the first request is sent and resets to 0 after 10 seconds.
  2. Per Day Rate Limit: Up to 50,000 requests per system within a single day. This window resets at midnight each day.

These rate limits are designed to ensure that no one user or application takes up more than their fair share of resources.

Make sure to respect the rate limits when you run into them and implement appropriate constraints into your application. If we notice any persistent and extensive disregard for these rate limits we reserve the right to take appropriate actions to ensure fair access for all users, which may include temporary suspension of API access privileges.
If you run into problems with these rate limits feel free to contact the pixx.io support.


Note: Please be aware that the specified rate limits are subject to change. We may adjust these limits as needed to optimize system performance and ensure fair resource allocation.

Rate Limit HTTP Headers

Each API response includes the following three headers which can be used to determine the current state for the rate limit:

  • X-RateLimit-Limit: This tells you the total number of requests allowed in the upcoming 10 seconds.
  • X-RateLimit-Remaining: You'll find out how many requests you can still make within the current 10-second window.
  • X-RateLimit-Reset: This lets you know when the rate limit counter will reset, so you can make more requests.

It is recommended to listen to these HTTP response headers in your code and adjust the speed of requests being sent accordingly to ensure your program does not run into rate limitations. If you run into one of the rate limits, all further requests will be answered by our API with a HTTP Status Code of 429 'Too Many Requests' until the rate limit windows reset.

Asynchronous Jobs

Asynchronous Jobs

Some of our available API Endpoints trigger actions that can take a bit longer to process, so our API cannot respond with the result right away.
These requests are stored in a MessageQueue and are worked on asynchronously in the background. The following available endpoints make use of this mechanism:

  • FilesPost: Uploading and processing large files.
  • FilesPut: Updating files that require server-side processing.
  • FilesDelete: Deleting files that may need additional server actions.
  • KeywordsUploadCSV: Uploading and processing keyword data from CSV files.
  • SynonymsUploadCSV: Uploading and processing synonym data from CSV files.

Asynchronous requests are used in scenarios where the server needs more time to handle the request or when the operation may be resource-intensive.

When you send one of these asynchronous requests, you receive a unique jobID that represents the job in the message queue. You can use this ID to track the job's progress:

  • JobGet Request: Retrieve detailed information about a specific job using the JobGet request. It is common to check the job at intervals to get information about the job's success or if an error occurred during processing.
curl --location --globoff \
  '{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/jobs/{your_job_id}' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {{your_api_key}}'

This request will provide you with insights into the job's current status, progress, and other relevant details.

Aborting a Job:

If you need to stop an ongoing asynchronous job, you can abort it:

  • JobAbort Request: Terminate a specific job.
curl --location --globoff --request PUT \
  '{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/jobs/{your_job_id}' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {{your_api_key}}'

This action will stop the job's execution and prevent further progress.

Job Status and Responses:

API responses related to job management provide useful information:

  • jobType: The type of the job being performed.
  • isStarted: Indicates if the job has started its execution.
  • isFinished: Indicates if the job has completed its execution.
  • progress: Shows the current progress of the job (usually as a percentage).
  • success: Indicates whether the job was successful or not.
  • error: Provides information about any errors encountered during the job.
  • jobData: Additional data related to the job's context and parameters.

These responses help you monitor the job's status and results. Take a look at the JobGet Endpoint in this documentation to get further information on how the jobData object exactly looks like for each different endpoint.

Metadata Fields

Metadata Fields

Standard Metadata Examples

Standard metadata fields provide commonly used information associated with digital assets. Some standard metadata examples include:

  • Title: The name or title of the asset.
  • Description: A brief description or summary of the asset's content.
  • Creator: The creator or author of the asset.
  • Keywords: Keywords or tags that help categorize and search for the asset.
  • Date Created: The date when the asset was created or captured.
  • Copyright Information: Details about copyright ownership and usage rights.

Furthermore, pixx.io offers support for various metadata field standards, including common standards such as EXIF and IPTC.

Custom Metadata Support

In addition to standard metadata, pixx.io also offers support for custom metadata fields. Custom metadata fields allow users to define and associate specific information that is unique to their organization or workflow.

Note: We are aware that the current way of handling Metadata Fields can be a bit confusing and needs further improvements in the future. We are actively working on improving the experience of working with metadata fields within our software and the API.
Response Fields and Attribute Filtering

Response Fields and Attribute Filtering

Most of the API's GET endpoints make use of a parameter called responseFields, which allows you to specify which attributes of a resource you want to receive in the response. This is particularly valuable when dealing with complex resources that contain a multitude of attributes.

Example:

curl --location --globoff \
  '{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files/{{your_file_id}}?responseFields=["id","fileName"]' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {{your_api_key}}'

The expected response of this request is:

{
  "success": true,
  "file": {
    "id": {file_id},
    "fileName": "{file_name}"
  }
}

Why Response Fields Matter

Utilizing response fields as a parameter is advantageous for:

  • Optimizing bandwidth by receiving only necessary data.
  • Improving response time by reducing payload size.
  • Enhancing security by limiting the exposure of sensitive attributes.

Object-Specific Response Fields

In addition to general response fields, the API offers object-specific response fields. For example, you might encounter "userResponseFields" when interacting with user-related resources. These object-specific fields allow you to further refine the attributes you receive in the response, narrowing down the data to only what's relevant to the specific object type you're working with.

curl --location --globoff \
  'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files/{file_id}?responseFields=["id","fileName","user"]&userResponseFields=["id","userName"]' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

The expected response of this request is:

{
  "success": true,
  "file": {
    "id": {file_id},
    "fileName": "{file_name}",
    "user": {
      "id": {user_id},
      "userName": "{user_name}"
    }
  }
}

Check the documentation for a specific endpoint to see exactly which response fields are valid for the request.

Pagination and Sorting

Pagination and Sorting

Pagination:

Pagination divides large sets of data into smaller chunks.

When making API requests, you'll work with these two main pagination parameters:

  • page: Specify the page number of the data you want.
  • pageSize: Determine how many items per page you'd like to receive.

These parameters allow you to control the quantity of data per response. Check the documentation of the specific endpoints to see the maximum allowed pageSize for this requests.

Sorting Data:

API responses can often be sorted based on certain attributes for better organization.

  • sortBy: Indicate the attribute by which you want to sort the data (e.g., name, createDate, etc.). Check the documentation of the specific endpoints to see all available sorting options.
  • sortDirection: Specify the sorting direction, either ascending or descending.

These parameters help you arrange the retrieved data in a meaningful order.

Combining Pagination and Sorting:

You can use pagination and sorting together to finely tailor your API requests:

curl --location \
  '{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files?pageSize=20&sortBy=uploadDate&page=4&sortDirection=ascending' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

In this example, you're requesting the second page of data, each page containing 20 items, sorted by the "uploadDate" attribute in ascending order.

Filters and Searching

Filters and Searching

The FilesGet request allows you to retrieve files based on various filter criteria. This functionality is essential for efficiently searching and accessing specific files within your pixx.io system.

Available Filters

You can apply one or more of the following filters to narrow down your search results:

  • collection: Filter files by their assigned collection.
  • colorspace: Search for files based on their color space like RGB or CMYK.
  • connectorAnd and connectorOr: Apply logical AND or OR operators to combine multiple filters to create complex search queries.
  • createDate: Filter files based on the date they were created.
  • creator: Search for files created by a specific user or creator.
  • description: Find files using descriptions or keywords associated with them.
  • directory: Filter files based on their directory or folder location.
  • fileExtension: Search for files with specific file extensions.
  • fileName: Search for files by their names.
  • fileState: Filter files based on their current state or status.
  • fileType: Search for files based on their file type (e.g., image, video).
  • height: Filter files by their height dimension.
  • width: Filter files by their width dimension.
  • keyword: Search for files using keywords associated with them.
  • isSubversion: Filter subversion files.
  • location: Search for files based on their geographical location.
  • metadataFieldDate: Filtering the metadata of files which have the edit type "date".
  • metadataFieldLocation: Filtering the metadata of files which have the edit type "location".
  • metadataFieldSelection: Filtering the metadata of files which have the edit type "selection".
  • metadataFieldText: filtering the metadata of files.
  • modifyDate: Filter files based on the date they were last modified.
  • uploadDate: Filter files based on the date they were uploaded.
  • orientation: Search for files based on their orientation (e.g., landscape, portrait).
  • rating: Find files with specific user ratings.
  • subject: Search for files by subject or topic.
  • user: Filter files based on the user.

Examples

Here is an example of how to use the FilesGet request with a connectorAnd Filter. In this example the filter searches for files which have "cat" AND "miau" in their description:

curl --location --globoff \
  'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files?page=1&pageSize=4&sortBy=uploadDate&filter={
    "filterType": "connectorAnd",
    "filters": [
      {
        "filterType": "description",
        "term": "cat",
        "exactMatch": false,
        "useSynonyms": true,
        "inverted": false
      },
      {
        "filterType": "description",
        "term": "miau",
        "exactMatch": false,
        "useSynonyms": true,
        "inverted": false
      }
    ],
    "inverted": false
  }' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

Here is an example of how to use the FilesGet request with a connectorOr Filter. In this example the filter searches for files which have "portrait" OR "widescreen" as orientation:

curl --location --globoff \
  'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files?page=1&pageSize=10&sortBy=fileName&filter={
    "filterType": "connectorOr",
    "filters": [
      {
        "filterType": "orientation",
        "orientation": "portrait",
        "inverted": false
      },
      {
        "filterType": "orientation",
        "orientation": "widescreen",
        "inverted": false
      }
    ],
    "inverted": false
  }' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'
Request Examples

Request Examples

File Upload

Upload Your File - FilesPost

Upload a file to the server using the FilesPost endpoint. Includes file details like the description and keywords such as the option to upload asynchronously.

curl --location 'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}' \
  --form 'file={your_file}' \
  --form 'description="This is the best juice in the world"' \
  --form 'keywords=["apple","orange"]' \
  --form 'asynchronousConversion="true"'

Expected Response:

{
  "success": true,
  "jobID": {job_id}
}

Track Progress - JobGet

Track a specific job to get more information about it. Common use is to send jobGet Requests in intervals until the job is finished.

curl --location --globoff \
  'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/jobs/{your_job_id}' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

If you want to learn more about asynchronous jobs, please refer to the Asynchronous Jobs section.



Retrieve File Information

Retrieve File Information - FileGet

Retrieve detailed information about a specific file using the FileGet endpoint. Specify response Fields to customize the output. In this case id and fileName should be returned in the response.

curl --location --globoff \
  'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/files/{file_id}?responseFields=["id","fileName"]' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

Expected Response

{
  "success": true,
  "file": {
    "id": {file_id},
    "fileName": "{file_name}"
  }
}


Create a Collection

Create a Collection - CollectionsPost

Create a new collection with a specific name, description, and a list of file IDs which should be included in the collection.

curl --location 'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/collections' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}' \
  --header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data-raw '{
      "name": "Beautiful Pictures",
      "description": "The most beautiful places in Bavaria.",
      "fileIDs": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
  }'

Expected Response

{
  "success": true,
  "id": {collection_id}
}


Retrieve Collection Information

Retrieve Collection Information - CollectionGet

Retrieve information about a specific collection. In this case it includes its name, modification date, and information about the user who created it like the id and the username.

curl --location --globoff \
  'https://{your_mediaspace_url}/api/v1/collections/{collection_id}?userResponseFields=["id","userName"]&responseFields=["id","name","modifyDate"]' \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer {your_api_key}'

Expected Response:

{
  "success": true,
  "collection": {
    "id": {collection_id},
    "name": "{collection_name}",
    "modifyDate": "{collection_modifyDate}",
    "user": {
      "id": {user_id},
      "userName": "{user_Name}"
    }
  }
}
info.title: pixx.io API

delete.description:
The collectionDelete endpoint removes one or more collections from your pixx.io workspace. Collections group assets logically and deleting them only removes the association, not the actual files.

Use Case

Use this to clean up or reorganize your collections without affecting the underlying assets.

Important Considerations

  • Files remain in the system; only their grouping is removed.
  • Ensure the user has appropriate permissions to delete controller_collections.

success.description: A bool with valid zero value "false".
success.title: Bool

get.description:
The collectionsGet endpoint retrieves a list of collections within the pixx.io system. Collections are used to organize files either statically or dynamically and can be filtered, sorted, and paginated.

Use Case

This is useful for rendering collection overviews or managing collections in administrative interfaces.

Performance Note

Use responseFields to limit the data returned and improve performance.

post.description:
The collectionsPost endpoint creates a new collection in pixx.io. Collections group related files either statically (by file list) or dynamically (via filters).

Use Case

Use this to organize assets for internal workflows, campaigns, or client-facing portals.

Important Considerations

  • For static collections, the name and fileIDs are required.
  • For dynamic collections, specify filterID; name is optional and defaults to the filter's name.
  • Collections can be shared with specific users or permission groups.
  • Set internalShareGlobally to allow access for all internal users.

assetNavigatorID.description: Optional. Associates the collection with an asset navigator.
assetNavigatorID.title: ID

createDate.description: A date and datetime in the format of YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS

filterID.description:
The ID of the filter to define a dynamic collection.
required if: isDynamic == true

subscribeCollection.description: Specifies if the user should subscribe to the collection after creation.

The collectionGet endpoint retrieves detailed information about a single collection. Collections are used to organize files, and this endpoint returns metadata and configuration settings.

Use Case

This is typically used in administrative or display contexts where you want to view how a collection is structured, what files it contains, or how it's shared.

put.description:
The collectionPut endpoint allows you to update existing collections of files within pixx.io, enabling you to manage and modify collection details as needed.

Endpoint Details

Endpoint: PUT /collections/{collection_id}

Authentication: Requires a valid API key.

Parameters: Replace {collection_id} with the specific ID of the collection you want to update.

Request Body

The request body should include the updated information for the collection. (see below)

Response

Upon successful update, the endpoint responds with a status code 200 OK.

Important Considerations

Updating a collection allows you to modify its details without affecting the associated files. Ensure you have the appropriate permissions and understand the impact before using this endpoint.

Request Structure

Here's how the request is structured:

schema.description: An ID is a number bigger than 0.
schema.title: ID

assetNavigatorID.description: Sets the asset navigator ID the collection should belong to.
assetNavigatorID.title: ID

description.description: Updates the description of the collection.
description.title: String

internalShareGlobally.description: Sets whether the collection is shared internally with all users.
internalShareGlobally.title: Bool

internalShareMessage.description: Sets an optional message displayed to users receiving internal sharing.
internalShareMessage.title: String

name.description: Updates the name of the collection.
name.title: Term

truncateAssetNavigatorID.description: Unset the assetNavigatorID (sets it to undefined).
truncateAssetNavigatorID.title: Bool

truncateDescription.description: Unset the description (sets it to undefined).
truncateDescription.title: Bool

userID.description: Specifies a user ID that should become the new owner of the collection.
userID.title: ID

success.description: A bool with valid zero value "false".
success.title: Bool

delete.description:
The directLinksDelete endpoint allows you to remove existing direct links to files within pixx.io.

Response

Upon successful deletion, the endpoint responds with a status code 200 OK.

Important Considerations

Using this endpoint enables you to remove direct links without affecting the underlying files. Ensure you have the necessary permissions to delete direct links.

Request Structure

Here's how the request is structured:

Imagine the following Directories: /one/two/three/four /one/two/three/five /six

For childID = 4 you receive the following response: parents: [1,2,3] children: [4,5]
schema.title: ID

Used to fetch the children and hierarchy below a specified parent directory ID.

Imagine the following Directories: /one/two/three/four /one/two/three/five /six

For parentID = 3 you receive the following response: parents: [1,2,3] children: [4,5]

This permission filter can be used to only include directories in the response where you have a specific permission for the files inside the directory.

The permission filter for the received directories. Note: Applying this filter with the value "uploadFiles" for example, does not imply that all returned directories have "uploadFiles = true" in its permissions-Object in the response. This is because the parents of the directories where the user has the permission to upload are also returned, so that the client connecting to the API can show the complete directory tree for its purpose.

Imagine the user only has the permission to upload in directory 4 and the filter "permission = uploadFiles" is applied.

The keywordsUploadCSV endpoint allows you to upload keyword data to pixx.io using a CSV file.

Use Case

This endpoint is useful when managing keywords in bulk, such as when importing keyword sets across teams. It enables you to batch-import keywords and bind specific permission groups to them.

Important Considerations

  • The file must be UTF-8 encoded.
  • The format must adhere to the specification:
  • Line 1 (optional): delimiter for Excel parsing.
  • Line 2 (optional): column headers - exactly "Keyword" and "PermissionGroups".
  • Line 3+: keyword and optional permission group assignments.
  • Group restrictions apply only when the keywordsBoundToPermissionGroups setting is enabled in pixxio.

The importantMetadataGet endpoint allows you to retrieve all metadata fields that are marked as important in your pixx.io system. An important metadata field can be one of the following types: - custom - custom metadata fields created by the user (can be of editType text, map, date, multi-selection etc.) - exif, iptc, xmp - also referred to as standard metadata fields - these are metadata fields that are automatically extracted from the file when it is uploaded. - internal - internal metadata fields that are used by pixxio to store information about the file (e.g., createDate, rating, fileSize) that serve a certain purpose in pixxio.

Use Case

Use this endpoint when building UIs that need to know which metadata fields are essential to a customer's workflows and are therefore part of the 'important' metadata. It helps in rendering metadata editors or search views efficiently.

Important Considerations

  • All metadata fields that are shown in the UI settings in the 'metadata' section are important metadata fields.
  • Important metadata fields appear in the file view at the top of the metadata list, they can be ordered (in the UI settings via drag & drop) so that they appear in a specific order.
  • Custom metadata fields are always important - these can be created freely without any restrictions in the UI settings.
  • Standard (exif, iptc, xmp) and internal metadata fields on the other hand can not be created completely freely, you can only choose from a list of available standard and internal metadata fields when adding them in the UI settings.
  • Each item in the response includes a top-level id field. This id does not represent the unique identifier of the actual metadata field itself. Instead, it identifies the important metadata entry — which is mainly used to control the ordering of metadata fields in the UI. If you need the true identifier of the underlying metadata field (e.g., for editing or referencing it elsewhere), you should use the metadata.id property inside the object.
  • When uploading a new file, only the standard (and some internal metadata fields) are automatically extracted from the file and filled in - custom metadata fields are not filled in automatically and always need to be filled in manually by the user or a script.
  • All other extracted standard metadata fields during upload that are not part of the important metadata fields are only visible in the file view at the very bottom after clicking on "show all metadata".

Request Structure

Here's how the request is structured:

schema.description:
Filters metadata fields by their isSearchable property.
If an important metadata is searchable was specified by the user when he added the metadata field in the UI
schema.title: Bool

success.description: A bool with valid zero value "false".
success.title: Bool

get.description:
The internalMetadataGet endpoint allows you to retrieve all available internal metadata fields. This list is static and does not change based on the files in your system or the metadata fields you have created in the settings. These metadata fields are typically used for internal logic or categorization, i.e. fileSize, fileType, rating, createDate, etc. Some of these are editable for a file (using the Files PUT endpoint), while others are not. When using the File PUT endpoint to modify an internal metadata field, you can usually find a top-level parameter that is used to modify a specific field for the file (i.e. rating). Therefore, you do not need to know the ID of the internal metadata field. This differs from standard / custom metadata modifying, because for these types one has to know the ID of the standard / custom metadata field and specify them in the array-parameters "metadataStandard" and "metadataCustom" of the Files PUT request in order to modify them for the file).

Use Case

This endpoint can be used to list all internal metadata fields and get their IDs. The IDs are only useful if you want to call the PUT Internal Metadata Endpoint to set a specific internal metadata field as important.

Request Structure

Here's how the request is structured:

schema.description: Filters internal metadata fields based on their name (case-insensitive partial match).

success.description: A bool with valid zero value "false".
success.title: Bool

get.description:
The standardMetadataGet endpoint allows you to retrieve the available standard metadata fields configured in your pixx.io instance. The term standard metadata refers to metadata fields of the standards EXIF, IPTC, and XMP. The returned metadata fields are not mediaspace-specific, this is a fixed list of standard metadata fields supported by pixx.io

Use Case

Use this endpoint when you need to query available standard metadata fields in order to get their ID that can for example be used for filtering files.

delete.description:
The modelReleasesDelete endpoint allows the deletion of a specific model release document associated with a file in the pixx.io system.

Use Case

This is commonly used when outdated or incorrect model release documents need to be removed, or when files are cleaned up for legal or organizational purposes.

Important Considerations

  • Deletion is permanent and cannot be undone.
  • Make sure the model release is not actively referenced before deletion.

success.title: Bool

post.description:
The synonymsUploadCSV endpoint allows you to upload synonym data to pixx.io using a CSV file.

Use Case

This endpoint is useful when managing synonyms in bulk, such as when importing synonym sets across teams. It enables you to batch-import synonyms.

Important Considerations

  • The file must be UTF-8 encoded.
  • The format must adhere to the specification:
  • Line 1 (optional): Delimiter for Excel parsing.
  • Line 2 (optional): Defines the names of the synonym columns. Existing synonym columns from the database are used when the name matches. New synonym columns are created when the specified column does not exist.
  • Line 3+: Contains synonym information, creating alternative keyword mappings.
  • Caution: Only a total of 20 synonym columns can be indexed in the database. Duplicate columns will result in an error.

Request Structure

Here's how the request is structured:

This is used when you want to provision new upload links for external contributors (e.g., photographers, agencies). It supports fine-grained control over release attachments, keywords, expiration settings, and notifications.
